description: Lingua di Roma antica, imposta dal latino classico (Cicero, Caesar) al latino volgare che si evolse nelle lingue romanze. Lingua liturgica cattolica e lingua scientifica europea fino al XVIII secolo.
ethical_notes: Latino imposto come lingua amministrativa post-conquista (Iberia, Gallia, Britannia), sostituendo iberico, celtico, brittonico. Sostituzione graduale, non violenta ma strutturale.

sources.citation: Adams, J.N. (2003). Bilingualism and the Latin Language. Cambridge University Press.

description: Ancient Greek — Mycenaean (Linear B) → Homeric → Classical (Attic, Ionic) → Koiné (Hellenistic lingua franca). Lingua della filosofia, scienza, Nuovo Testamento.

sources.citation: Horrocks, G. (2010). Greek: A History of the Language and Its Speakers. Wiley.

description: Vedic Sanskrit (Rig Veda c.1500 BCE) → Classical Sanskrit (Panini c.4th c. BCE). Liturgical language for Hinduism, Buddhism, Jainism. Grammatically fossilized by Panini's Ashtadhyayi.

sources.citation: Cardona, G. (2007). The Indo-Aryan Languages. Routledge.

description: Akkadian — lingua semitica dei regni di Akkad, Babylon, Assyria. Dialetti: Old Akkadian, Babylonian, Assyrian. Lingua franca Near East Bronze Age. Deciphered by Rawlinson (1850s) via Behistun inscription.

sources.citation: Huehnergard, J. (2011). A Grammar of Akkadian. Eisenbrauns.

description: Ancient Egyptian — Old Egyptian (3200-2000 BCE) → Middle Egyptian (classical, 2000-1350) → Late Egyptian → Demotic → Coptic (liturgical Christian Egyptian until Arabic displacement). Decifrato Champollion 1822 via Rosetta Stone.
ethical_notes: Coptic (last stage) ridotto a liturgico da Arab conquest (641 CE). Scomparsa complet a parlante native ~11th-17th c. Revival movement minore.

sources.citation: Loprieno, A. (1995). Ancient Egyptian: A Linguistic Introduction. Cambridge.

description: Old Chinese — lingua dei testi oracolari (1250 BCE) e dei Classici (Shijing, Analects, Daodejing). Reconstruction per cinese medio attraverso varianti dialettali e prestiti vietnamiti/coreani.

sources.citation: Baxter, W. & Sagart, L. (2014). Old Chinese: A New Reconstruction. Oxford.

region_name: China (literary lingua franca across East Asia)
description: Classical / Literary Chinese — lingua letteraria impiegata per 2500 anni. Scritta, non parlata. Usata anche in Korea (Hanmun), Japan (Kanbun), Vietnam (Hán văn). Sostituita dal vernacolare baihua nel 1912 con la Quarta Maggio.

sources.citation: Norman, J. (1988). Chinese. Cambridge.

description: Classical / Modern Standard Arabic — lingua del Corano (7th c.), poi lingua franca dell'espansione islamica. Oggi lingua liturgica + MSA parlata da ~400M attraverso dialetti regionali.
ethical_notes: Arabizzazione linguistica post-conquista islamica ha causato scomparsa di copto in Egypt, aramaico in Levant, berbero imazighen in N-Africa (revival contemporaneo).

sources.citation: Versteegh, K. (2014). The Arabic Language. Edinburgh.

description: Biblical Hebrew (1000-500 BCE) → Mishnaic → Medieval (liturgical/Mishnaic) → Modern (Ivrit revival da Ben-Yehuda 19th c., lingua ufficiale Israele 1948). Caso unico di revival successful di lingua 'morta'.
ethical_notes: Revival Hebrew soppresso Yiddish e Ladino nella diaspora israeliana (1948+) — controverso trade-off identitario.

sources.citation: Sáenz-Badillos, A. (1993). A History of the Hebrew Language. Cambridge.

description: Aramaic — lingua franca Near East 700 BCE - 700 CE (Persian Empire, post-exilic Israel, Jesus era). Syriac (Christian Aramaic) ancora liturgico Churches of the East. Neo-Aramaic dialects (Assyrian, Mandaic) endangered.
ethical_notes: Aramaic communities target di pulizie etniche: Simele 1933 (Iraqi Assyrians), Sayfo 1914-1920 (Syriacs genocide), ISIS displacement 2014+ (Mosul, Nineveh). UNESCO endangered list.

sources.citation: Gzella, H. (2015). A Cultural History of Aramaic. Brill.

description: Gaulish (continental Celtic) — parlato in Gallia, Iberia, N-Italia. Attestato in ~150 iscrizioni. Displaced da latino sotto Roman conquista (58-51 BCE). Britannic Celtic (Brythonic) → sopravvissuto come Welsh, Cornish, Breton.
ethical_notes: Displacement by Latin conquest fu amministrativo, non violento diretto. Ma Celtic Breton ancora soppresso da French state fino 1950s (sanzioni scolastiche).

sources.citation: Lambert, P.-Y. (1994). La langue gauloise. Errance.

description: Etruscan — lingua non-indoeuropea, isolate (possibile parente Rhaetic + Lemnian = Tyrsenian). ~13,000 iscrizioni superstiti. Parzialmente decifrato via liber linteus (Zagabria mummy wrappings).
ethical_notes: Assorbimento progressivo da romani post-396 BCE (Veii). Sprachtod circa 1 BCE. Storia culturale diversa da quella di conquista diretta.

sources.citation: Wallace, R. (2008). Zikh Rasna: A Manual of the Etruscan Language. Beech Stave.

description: Classical Nahuatl — lingua Mexica / Aztec Empire. Lingua franca Mesoamerican post-1521 (Spanish missionaries documented it). Modern Nahuatl dialects (~1.7M speakers) endangered.
ethical_notes: Spanish conquest 1521 conservò Nahuatl come lingua francese coloniale per mezzo secolo (Sahagún's Florentine Codex 1569). Poi graduale suppression. 20th c. castellanización eliminò trasmissione inter-generazionale. UNESCO sei classi endangered.

sources.citation: Lockhart, J. (2001). Nahuatl as Written. Stanford.

region_name: Andes (Peru, Bolivia, Ecuador, Colombia, Argentina, Chile)
description: Quechua — lingua franca Tawantinsuyu (Inca Empire). 10M parlanti moderni attraverso ~40 dialetti. Official lingua in Peru (1975), Bolivia (2009), Ecuador. Resilience impressionante post-conquista.
ethical_notes: Spanish castellanización campaigns 17th-20th c. sopressero Quechua in educazione. Still marginal in scuole rurali. Revival con riconoscimento costituzionale ma declino persistente urbano.

sources.citation: Cerrón-Palomino, R. (1987). Lingüística quechua. Centro Bartolomé de las Casas.

description: Maya language family — ~32 lingue moderne, ~6M parlanti (K'iche' 1.9M, Yucatec 800K). Classical Maya (300-900 CE) decifratura glifica (Knorozov 1950s → Coe 1992).
ethical_notes: Bishop Diego de Landa 1562 Mani book-burning distrusse la maggior parte dei codici maya. Solo 4 sopravvissuti (Dresden, Madrid, Paris, Grolier). Revival post-Civil War Guatemala 1996+; resistenza linguistic ongoing.

sources.citation: Coe, M.D. (1992). Breaking the Maya Code. Thames & Hudson.

description: PIE — lingua ancestrale ricostruita di tutte le lingue indoeuropee (latino, greco, sanscrito, slavo, germanico, celtico, albanese, armeno, iranico, tocarico). Ricostruzione comparative method (Bopp 1816, Brugmann 1880s). Kurgan hypothesis (Marija Gimbutas) per Urheimat.
ethical_notes: Uso nazi di 'Aryan' come categoria razziale (20th c.) basato su distorsione di PIE research. Tom Trautmann Aryan Debate documenta abuso. Ricerca genuina evita cognate language ↔ racial group conflation.

sources.citation: Mallory, J.P. & Adams, D.Q. (2006). The Oxford Introduction to Proto-Indo-European.

description: Gothic — lingua germanica orientale. Wulfila's Bibbia translation 4th c. CE. Ostrogothic Italia + Visigothic Iberia. Ultima attestazione: Crimean Gothic osservato by Busbecq 1562 (16th c. relict).

sources.citation: Braune, W. & Heidermanns, F. (2004). Gotische Grammatik. Niemeyer.

region_name: Scandinavia + Viking diaspora (Iceland, Greenland, Ireland, Normandy)
description: Old Norse / Old Icelandic — lingua Viking age. Eddic poetry + prose sagas (Snorri Sturluson 13th c.). Evoluta in Icelandic, Faroese, Norwegian, Danish, Swedish, Norn (Orkney/Shetland extinct 19th c.).

sources.citation: Faulkes, A. (1995). Edda. Everyman.

description: Avestan — lingua liturgica zoroastriana. Gathas (Zarathustra c.1700-1300 BCE) oldest. Oral transmission fino al codifica sasanide (3rd-7th c. CE). Ancora uso liturgico tra parsi (India) e zoroastriani (Iran).

sources.citation: Kellens, J. (1989). Avestan Grammar. Harvard.

description: Old Persian — lingua regale Achaemenid. Deciphered via Behistun Trilingual (Rawlinson 1835-1846). Direct ancestor di Middle Persian (Pahlavi) → Modern Persian (Farsi / Dari / Tajik).

sources.citation: Kent, R.G. (1953). Old Persian: Grammar, Texts, Lexicon. American Oriental Society.

description: Kurdish — macrolanguage: Kurmanji (NW), Sorani (Central), Pehlewani (S). ~30M speakers. Documentato come lingua letteraria 10th c. (Baba Tahir, Ehmed Xani).
ethical_notes: Turkish ban 1923-1991 (parola 'Kurd' illegal), Iraqi Arabization (Ba'ath Party 1970s+, Halabja 1988 chemical attack ~5000 dead), Iranian + Syrian restrictions ongoing. PKK period armato. Revival educazione post-2003 (Kurdistan Region Iraq).

sources.citation: Thackston, W. M. (2006). Sorani Kurdish: A Reference Grammar. Harvard.

description: Classical Tibetan (yig skad) — liturgical Vajrayana Buddhist. Modern Lhasa Tibetan + Amdo + Kham. ~6M speakers. Alphabet introdotto da Thonmi Sambhota ~650 CE.
ethical_notes: Chinese PRC post-1959 language restrictions in schools, Sinification policies. Self-immolation protests 2011+. Boarding school removals documented post-2020.

sources.citation: Beyer, S. (1992). The Classical Tibetan Language. SUNY.

description: Old English — Beowulf, Anglo-Saxon Chronicle. Dialetti West Saxon (Winchester court), Mercian, Northumbrian, Kentish. Norman Conquest 1066 ended scribal supremacy → Middle English.
ethical_notes: Norman conquest (1066) impose French at court; Old English reduced to lower-class vernacular for ~300 years. Renaissance of English only 14th c. (Chaucer).

sources.citation: Hogg, R. (2002). An Introduction to Old English. Edinburgh.

description: Lingua liturgica Slava ortodossa. Cyrill e Metodio 863 CE, tradotta Bibbia. Glagolitic alphabet → Cyrillic (Kliment di Ohrid). Ancora lingua liturgica nelle chiese ortodosse slave.

sources.citation: Lunt, H. (2001). Old Church Slavonic Grammar. De Gruyter.

description: Nahuatl Pipil — southern Uto-Aztecan branch. ~200 native speakers (2015). Genocide del 1932 El Salvador (La Matanza) sotto Martínez eliminò ~25,000 Pipil+Indigenous, imposing Spanish+machismo. Survival comes through determined post-genocide generations.
ethical_notes: ETHICS-007 explicit: La Matanza 1932 ~25,000-40,000 Pipil+Indigenous mass killed by Martinez dictatorship. Linguistic genocide (many stopped speaking Pipil from fear). Revival programs only post-1992 peace accords.

sources.citation: Campbell, L. (1985). The Pipil Language of El Salvador. Mouton.

description: Hawaiian — Polynesian settlement ~1000 CE. Writing imposed by missionaries 1820s. 1896 Territory Law banned Hawaiian in schools (25,000 → <1000 native speakers by 1970s). Renaissance 1978+ (constitution), ʻAha Pūnana Leo immersion schools 1983.
ethical_notes: ETHICS-009 analog: US colonial linguicide 1896-1978 (corporal punishment in schools for speaking Hawaiian). Revival one of most successful 20th c. cases. Now co-official with English (since 1978).

sources.citation: Pukui, M.K. & Elbert, S.H. (1986). Hawaiian Dictionary. Hawaii UP.

description: Ainu — lingua isolate dell'Hokkaido (e storicamente Sakhalin + Kuriles). ~10 fluent speakers (2010 estimate). Japanese Meiji 1869 policy di assimilazione. Ainu Cultural Promotion Act 1997 riconobbe status indigeno.
ethical_notes: Japanese colonial linguicide 1869-1945 (Hokkaido Former Aboriginal Protection Act 1899 imposto giapponese). Ainu Association 1946. Indigenous People Act 2019 finally formal recognition.

sources.citation: Tamura, S. (2000). The Ainu Language. Sanseido.

description: Swahili — lingua franca East Africa costal trade. Emerged ~900 CE da contatto Bantu-Arabic. ~150M speakers (L1 + L2). Ufficiale Tanzania, Kenya, Uganda, Rwanda, DRC (co-lingua), African Union (2004).

sources.citation: Nurse, D. & Hinnebusch, T. (1993). Swahili and Sabaki. Berkeley.

description: Polish — Christianization 966 CE marked beginning documentazione. Jan Kochanowski 16th c. literary peak. Partitions era (1795-1918) restrictions. Totalmente reinstated 1918 + again 1989.
ethical_notes: Nazi occupation 1939-1945 targeted intelligentsia (Polish elite), destruction of libraries, university closures. ~6M Polish deaths (3M Jewish, 3M ethnic Polish).

sources.citation: Swan, O.E. (2002). A Grammar of Contemporary Polish. Slavica.
